title: The Trigger in Being Told to Calm Down
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: For a nervous system wired by trauma, being told to 'calm down' is a trigger. Learn why this happens and how to build an internal sense of safety.
imageDescription: A delicate piece of pottery, repaired with shimmering gold, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, soft morning light illuminates the intricate golden cracks, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing.
htmlContent:
The invalidation is not the point. The point is the sudden, terrifying regression to a state where your entire nervous system screams that you are, once again, fundamentally misunderstood and unsafe in the one place you are supposed to be seen.

The Anatomy of a Well-Intentioned Wound

For a nervous system wired by complex trauma, the phrase “calm down” is not an invitation to peace; it is a neurological alarm bell. It signals a genuine misattunement, a gap between our internal reality and the other person’s perception that feels indistinguishable from the original relational ruptures that laid down these traumatic pathways in the first place. It’s a moment that communicates, with devastating efficiency, that our experience is too much, too inconvenient, or simply not valid. The body, in its infinite and sometimes brutal wisdom, remembers the times when expressing distress led to punishment, abandonment, or dismissal, and it reacts accordingly - not with logic, but with a surge of defensive energy designed for survival. This is the nervous system doing its job perfectly, just a job that is no longer relevant to the present circumstances. It's a survival mechanism from a bygone era of our lives, still operating on outdated software.

This isn’t a cognitive process; it’s a somatic one. The well-meaning friend or partner is not responding to the present-moment adult; they are unknowingly speaking directly to the young part of us that was taught that its feelings were a problem to be solved. And so, the body does what it learned to do: it either shuts down into a state of frozen collapse or escalates into a fight-or-flight response, desperately trying to make its internal state understood. The work, then, is not to learn to “calm down” on command, but to understand the architecture of this trigger and begin the slow, deliberate work of building a new, internal sense of safety that doesn’t depend on external validation. It’s about learning to offer ourselves the attunement we never received, a process that begins with honoring the body’s ancient, intelligent, and deeply protective response. This road is central to understanding the space of complex trauma and its pervasive impact on our relational lives.

The Echo of Dismissal in the Nervous System

When we are told to calm down, what the body actually hears is a confirmation of its deepest fear: “Your reality is not real.” What emerges is not hyperbole; it is the lived, cellular experience of developmental trauma. The implicit demand to regulate our emotional state for the comfort of another person mirrors the very dynamic that created the trauma... the need to suppress our own authentic feelings to maintain a fragile connection with a caregiver. As Bessel van der Kolk’s work so powerfully illustrates, the body keeps the score, and the score is a running tally of every moment our internal world was denied, dismissed, or punished. The trigger isn’t in the words themselves, but in the relational echo they produce, a resonance that vibrates through the nervous system and activates the well-worn pathways of relational dread. It’s a reminder of the times we had to choose between our truth and our attachment, and the body remembers which one it had to sacrifice to survive. This is not a memory stored in narrative, but a memory stored in the sinews, in the gut, in the very rhythm of our breath.

A simple grounding stone that fits in your pocket is Worry Stone - Amethyst (paid link).

The Tyranny of Cognitive Control

Our culture is obsessed with the idea of mind over matter, the belief that with enough willpower, we can think our way out of any discomfort. That becomes a particularly cruel fiction to impose on someone with a trauma history. The very nature of trauma is that it bypasses the thinking mind and lodges itself in the non-verbal, sensory world of the body. The instruction to “calm down” is a demand for cognitive control over a process that is, by its very nature, pre-cognitive. It’s like telling someone to use a map to find their way out of a room when the room is on fire. The immediate, sensory reality of the fire is what matters, not the abstract representation of the building’s layout. The body is in a state of five-alarm fire, and the well-meaning person is asking it to consult a floor plan. This insistence on top-down control is a form of gaslighting, however unintentional. It subtly reinforces the idea that our bodily experience is unreliable and that we should trust the cognitive assessments of others over our own internal signals.

Bear with me on this one.

For a deeper exploration of these themes, see more on this from Kalesh.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link).

Building the Internal Witness

If we cannot think our way into safety, and if external validation is unreliable, what is the path forward? The work lies in cultivating an internal witness, a part of our consciousness that can observe the storm of the trauma response without being swept away by it. That becomes not a state of detachment, but one of real, compassionate engagement with our own internal world. It is the part of us that can say, “Ah, there it is. The familiar wave of panic. The tightness in the chest. The urge to flee.” This witness doesn’t judge the reaction or try to stop it. It simply notices. And in that noticing, a sliver of space is created. It’s the gap between the stimulus ~ the trigger ~ and the response. It’s in this space that a new possibility can emerge. This is the space where freedom is born, not as an escape from our reality, but as the capacity to be present with it, whatever it may be.

This practice of internal witnessing is what allows us to begin to untangle the past from the present. The body’s reaction is real, but the threat it is responding to is often an echo. The witness can learn to recognize the echo for what it is. It can hold the body’s fear with compassion while also gently reminding it of the present reality: “We are safe now. That was then. What emerges is now.” This is not an intellectual exercise. It is a felt sense, a deep, somatic knowing that begins to permeate the system over time. It is the slow, patient work of reparenting ourselves, of offering the steady, attuned presence that we never received. It is the work of becoming our own sanctuary. This is not about building a fortress, but about cultivating a garden, a place within ourselves where we can rest, even when the world outside is stormy.

Navigating the Relational Field

While the core of the work is internal, we do not heal in a vacuum. We heal in relationship - to ourselves, and eventually, to others. The trigger of being told to “calm down” is, at its heart, a relational problem, and so its healing must also have a relational component. This does not mean demanding that others change for us. It means learning to communicate our needs and boundaries in a way that is both clear and compassionate. It means moving from a place of reactive defense to one of responsive self-advocacy. What emerges is perhaps one of the most challenging and rewarding aspects of the healing work, as it requires us to bring our newfound internal safety into the unpredictable world of human connection.

A singing bowl that some find grounding through vibration is Singing Bowl Set (paid link).

This might look like saying to a loved one, in a moment of calm, “I want to share something with you about how my nervous system works. When I’m feeling overwhelmed, hearing the words ‘calm down’ actually makes me feel more panicked. What would be really helpful for me in those moments is if you could just sit with me, or ask if I’d like some space.” What we find here is not a confrontation. It is an invitation. It is an act of genuine vulnerability and trust, both in ourselves and in the other person. It is sharing the user manual for our nervous system, a manual that we are only just learning to read ourselves. For those who are truly committed to our well-being, this information is a gift, a roadmap to loving us better. For those who are unwilling or unable to meet us in this, that, too, is valuable information, a clarification of the limits of that particular relationship. This is not about testing people, but about gathering data, about learning who in our lives has the capacity for the kind of attunement that fosters healing, a crucial aspect of building a trauma-informed support system.

Think about that for a second.

What the Silence Holds

Healing from complex trauma is not a process of getting rid of our triggers. It is a process of changing our relationship to them. The trigger itself ~ the phrase “calm down” ~ may always produce a flicker of activation. The work is to ensure that the flicker does not have to become a forest fire. That becomes the architecture of returning: the ability to be thrown from our center and to find our way back, a little more quickly, a little more gently, each time. It is the cultivation of a nervous system that is not rigid or brittle, but resilient and flexible, a system that has the capacity to bend without breaking. It is the difference between a glass that shatters on impact and a willow branch that bends in the wind and returns to its shape.

An herbal option that some find soothing for anxiety is Gaia Herbs Passionflower (paid link).

Are you willing to be uncomfortable long enough for something to shift?
